original_abstract: While discourses surrounding equity, diversity, inclusion, and Indigenization (EDI&I) have become highly visible within large organizations like post-secondary institutions, research continues to document only modest progress toward these stated goals. Obstacles to meaningful change stem largely from the structural nature of racist and sexist domination, which remains deeply embedded in both the formal and informal norms of institutional life. Drawing on a close examination of two university-based EDI&I initiatives and direct experiences of "pushback," this article conceptualizes the phenomenon of institutional gaslighting. This dynamic occurs when universities paradoxically champion EDI&I rhetoric while simultaneously deploying strategies that maintain systemic inequalities and stifle structural reform. By theorizing this tension, we aim to provide a framework for identifying and addressing forms of institutional resistance, offering a pathway to bridge the gap between high-level values and transformative practice within large institutions.
humanized_abstract: In recent years, equity, diversity, inclusion, and Indigenization (EDI&I) have become prominent topics in the rhetoric of large institutions such as colleges and universities. Despite the prominent coverage in media outlets and internal university documents, very little research has produced tangible evidence of real change occurring on campus. One major barrier to such change is that the very systems that perpetuate racist and sexist violence and marginalization are built into the very structures and practices of modern institutions, and are therefore invisible as social currency, but present in hundreds of implicit and explicit ways that reinforce discrimination and hinder change. Taking the authors’ own experience of a pair of campaigns to promote EDI&I at the university in which they work as a case study, and combining this with a close reading of the ways in which those behind such campaigns respond to attempts to name the inevitable “pushback” that occurs when people advocate for “change from below”, this article theorizes a phenomenon of “institutional gaslighting”, by which the institutions in question communicate to those working from within them that the very values that the university has “adopted” — equity, diversity, inclusion, and Indigenization — are irrelevant and lacking in any tangible impact on everyday life, in the process undermining the possibility of structural change while valorizing their EDI&I agendas in rhetoric and ideology. Theorizing this phenomenon offers the prospect of developing tools for identifying “pushback’ in a variety of forms, and for bridging the ideological and rhetorical gap between ideals of social justice in the rhetoric of institutions and the actual practices and daily life on campus.
